title: Alan Lomaxãs Massive Music Archive Is Online: Features 20,000 Historic Blues & Folk Recordings
description:
in Music |   	March 19th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
A huge treaôÙsure trove of songs and interôÙviews recordôÙed by the legôÙendary folkôÙlorist Alan L
body:
in Music |   	March 19th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
A huge treaôÙsure trove of songs and interôÙviews recordôÙed by the legôÙendary folkôÙlorist Alan Lomax from the 1940s into the 1990s has been digôÙiôÙtized and made availôÙable online for free lisôÙtenôÙing. The AssoôÙciôÙaôÙtion for CulôÙturôÙal EquiôÙty, a nonôÙprofôÙit orgaôÙniôÙzaôÙtion foundôÙed by Lomax in the 1980s, has postôÙed some 20,000 recordôÙings.
ãFor the first time,ã CulôÙturôÙal EquiôÙty ExecôÙuôÙtive DirecôÙtor Don FlemôÙing told NPRãs Joel Rose, ãeveryôÙthing that weãve digôÙiôÙtized of Alanãs field recordôÙing trips are online, on our Web site. Itãs every take, all the way through. False takes, interôÙviews, music.ã
Itãs an amazôÙing resource. For a quick taste, here are a few examôÙples from one of the best-known areas of Lomaxãs research, his recordôÙings of traôÙdiôÙtionôÙal African AmerôÙiôÙcan culôÙture:

ãJohn HenôÙryã sungô by prisôÙonôÙers at the MisôÙsisôÙsipôÙpi State PenôÙiôÙtenôÙtiary, ParchôÙman Farm, in 1947.
ãCome Up Horsey,ã a chilôÙdrenãs lulôÙlaôÙby sung in 1948 by Vera Hall, whose mothôÙer was a slave.
ãIn a ShanôÙty in Old ShanôÙty Townã perôÙformed by Big Bill Broonzy, 1952.
ãStoôÙry of a slave who asked the devôÙil to take his masôÙter,ã told by Bessie Jones in 1961.

But thatãs just scratchôÙing the surôÙface of whatãs inside the enorôÙmous archive. Lomaxãs work extendôÙed far beyond the Deep South, into othôÙer areas and culôÙtures of AmerôÙiôÙca, the Caribbean, Europe and Asia. ãHe believed that all culôÙtures should be looked at on an even playôÙing field,ã his daughôÙter Anna Lomax Wood told NPR. ãNot that theyãre all alike. But they should be givôÙen the same digôÙniôÙty, or they had the same digôÙniôÙty and worth as any othôÙer.ã
You can lisôÙten to Roseãs pieceô about the archive on the NPR webôÙsite, as well as a 1990 interôÙview with Lomax by TerôÙry Gross of Fresh Air, which includes samôÙple recordôÙings from Woody Guthrie, JelôÙly Roll MorôÙton, Lead BelôÙly and MisôÙsisôÙsipôÙpi Fred McDowôÙell. To dive into the Lomax audio archive, you can search the vast colôÙlecôÙtion by artist, date, genre, counôÙtry and othôÙer catôÙeôÙgories.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post origôÙiôÙnalôÙly appeared on our site in March 2012.

If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. title: In Her Final Reflections, Jane Goodall Issues a Warning: ãWithout Hope, We Fall Into Apathyã
description:
in Life, Science |   	March 18th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

For many of us, Jane Goodall was one of those culôÙturôÙal figôÙures who seemed alway
body:
in Life, Science |   	March 18th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

For many of us, Jane Goodall was one of those culôÙturôÙal figôÙures who seemed always to have been around, and on some levôÙel, made us feel like she always would be. But of course, no human being lives forôÙevôÙer, no matôÙter how wideôÙly admired. Goodall made her own deparôÙture last fall, in the midôÙdle of an AmerôÙiôÙcan speakôÙing tour, at the age of 91. Just two days thereôÙafter, she appeared as the guest on the preôÙmiere of NetôÙflixãs Famous Last Words, a proôÙgram conôÙsistôÙing of interôÙviews conôÙductôÙed expressôÙly to air only after the interôÙvieôÙweeãs death. In the clip above, the showãs host, TV writer-direcôÙtor-proôÙducôÙer Brad Falchuk, asks her an outôÙwardôÙly simôÙple quesôÙtion: ãWho would you say you were?ã
Goodall describes herôÙself as ãsomeôÙbody sent to this world to try to give peoôÙple hope in dark times, because withôÙout hope, we fall into apaôÙthy and do nothôÙing, and in the dark times that we are livôÙing in now, if peoôÙple donãt have hope, weãre doomed. How can we bring litôÙtle chilôÙdren into this dark world weãve creôÙatôÙed and let them be surôÙroundôÙed by peoôÙple whoãve givôÙen up? So even if this is the end of humanôÙiôÙty as we know it, letãs fight to the very end. Letãs let the chilôÙdren know that there is hope, if they get togethôÙer. And even if it becomes imposôÙsiôÙble for anyôÙbody, itãs betôÙter to go on fightôÙing to the end than just to give up and say, ãOkay.ãô ã These are fine words, though it may surôÙprise some of us that they make no menôÙtion of chimps.
Though she became famous as a priôÙmaôÙtolôÙoôÙgist, and specifôÙiôÙcalôÙly as an expert on chimôÙpanzees, Goodall devotôÙed much enerôÙgy in her latôÙer decades to takôÙing action on broadôÙer causôÙes. These includôÙed enviôÙronôÙmenôÙtal conôÙserôÙvaôÙtion and the secuôÙriôÙty of life on Earth itself, which she saw as imperôÙiled by the actions of cerôÙtain govôÙernôÙments and politôÙiôÙcal actors. When Falchuk asks who she doesôÙnãt like, she expressôÙes her desire to send perôÙmaôÙnentôÙly into space Elon Musk, DonôÙald Trump and ãsome of his real supôÙportôÙers,ã Vladimir Putin, Xi JinôÙping, BenôÙjamin Netanyahu and ãhis far-right govôÙernôÙment.ã That answer may get us wonôÙderôÙing whether the resultôÙing powôÙer vacôÙuôÙums would be filled by more or less savory charôÙacôÙters. The entire interôÙview will leave us with anothôÙer, perôÙhaps more trouôÙbling quesôÙtion: who, today, could be suitôÙed to assume the place in pubôÙlic life left behind by Jane Goodall?

Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Restrained Emotions Simmer in Shinsuke Inoue's Tender Wood Sculptures
description: Around a decade ago, Shinsuke Inoue sourced a piece of Japanese wood and carved a depiction of his child, ãwanting to preserve their likeness in t
body:
Around a decade ago, Shinsuke Inoue sourced a piece of Japanese wood and carved a depiction of his child, ãwanting to preserve their likeness in three dimensions,ã the artist tells Colossal. The affectionate expression of a loved one in sculptural form spurred a new passion for woodcarving, specifically with an emphasis on the human figure.
Inoueãs pieces possess a kind of elemental groundedness or gravity that makes their restrained, sometimes hard-to-read expressions remarkably alluring. The figures often look straight ahead, and at the right angle, they make powerful eye contact with the viewer. And not unlike the way a small, meaningful smile or tiny frown can emerge from the most minute twitch of facial muscles, the striking characters are physically diminutive, but their inner emotional worlds are infinite.

Inoue works intuitively, allowing the materialãs natural qualities to guide his hand. ãIô have virtually no idea what the finished piece will look like until I actually begin working with the wood,ã he says. ãAs a result, the form often emerges as I carve, and I frequently change my plans midway through the process. Naturally, I keep the many failures a secret.ã He always carves using hand tools and rarely titles the pieces.
The artist also references people heãs close to, along with strangers he passes on the street or sees photographs of, but his sculptures arenãt realistic depictions of specific individuals. Instead, Inoue concentrates on capturing a kind of universal expression of ãthe very essence of human existenceãÎ I hope that the inherent appeal of the wood, combined with its form and color, resonates to convey the essence of humanity itself.ã
See more on Instagram.

schema.headline: Restrained Emotions Simmer in Shinsuke Inoue's Tender Wood Sculptures

title: Marc Fornes' New Sculptural Pavilion Reimagines the Architectural Folly
description: A bold new structure has appeared in Cary Park in Cary, North Carolina: the latest sculptural pavilion by Marc Fornes / THEVERYMANY. The work is t
body:
A bold new structure has appeared in Cary Park in Cary, North Carolina: the latest sculptural pavilion by Marc Fornes / THEVERYMANY. The work is titled ãLãIle Folie,ã which nods to the architectural tradition of the folly, a landscape feature that was all the rage with wealthy estate owners in the 18th and 19th centuries.
Often nostalgic and resembling ruined miniature castles or bucolic village buildings, follies were generally non-functional and conceived as pure decoration. Fornes, however, reimagines this practice with an eye toward the future rather than the past. The pavilion ãgives this tradition a contemporary meaning:ô memorable, playful, and slightly surreal,ã says a statement.

Fornes is known for creating high-tech structures made from thousands of individual facets, blurring the distinction between architecture and sculpture. Situated along a boardwalk and perched over a pond, the gleaming white pavilion invites visitors to pause and appreciate their natural surroundings from a contemporary landmark.
ãConstructed from ultra-thin folded aluminum panels, each piece is digitally fabricated and precisely riveted into place,ã says a statement. ãThere is no hidden frame; the skin is the structure. Thousands of perforations filter sunlight into delicate patterns, turning the canopy into an ever-changing atmosphere of shadow and shimmer.ã
See more on THEVERYMANYãs Instagram and Vimeo.

schema.headline: Marc Fornes' New Sculptural Pavilion Reimagines the Architectural Folly

March 18, 2026


Graph paper is commonly used for plotting, well, graphs,
body:
ãArt in Veinsã (2025). All images courtesy of Pejac, shared with permission
March 18, 2026


Graph paper is commonly used for plotting, well, graphs, plus other spatial and mathematical visualizations. But for Pejac, its potential goes way beyond a two-dimensional gridded surface. The artist, who is known for his trompe-lãéil paintings and playful street art interventions, often turns to the precise geometry of gridded sketchbooks in order to challenge perception and think instead about depth and movement.
From a group of kids tossing snowballsãwait, theyãre cubes from the grid itselfãto a construction worker carving out a silhouette of the famous Sistine Chapel motif of God and Adamãs hands touching, Pejac challenges our sense of space and the possibilities of the ãblank slate.ã Find more on Instagram.
